questions.description: (A) Having regards to both its role and its composition, analyse… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. (A) Having regards to both its role and its composition, analyse critically whether the Commission effectively 'promotes the general interest of the Union' as required by Article 17(1) TEU. Your answer should particularly focus on the Commission's power of initiative in the legislative process, its function in ensuring the application of measures adopted by the Union institutions and its role as 'guardian of the Treaties'. It should also refer to relevant case law of the Union judicature. OR (B) By reference particularly to Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice compare and contrast: i. the legal nature of a 'regulation', a 'directive' and a 'decision' in Union law; ii. the core differences between a 'legislative act' and a 'non legislative act' in Union law as regards both their nature and the means by which they are adopted; iii. the difference between 'delegated' acts and 'implementing' acts in Union law.

questions.description: (A) 'The right to an effective remedy is a general principle of EU law… Constitutional, Fundamental Rights.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. (A) 'The right to an effective remedy is a general principle of EU law that exists within the context of the multi-level constitutional system of the EU' (Hoffmann, HCH in his essay Article 47- Right to an effective remedy' in the EU charter of Fundamental Rights -- A commentary (Peers, Hervey, Kenner & Ward editors, 2014) at page 1211. By reference, in particular, to relevant case-law and the scope of the right conferred by Article 47 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU as well as its import for the principles of effectiveness and equivalence, discuss critically the view expressed by Professor Hoffmann as to the import of the right to an effective remedy. OR (B) 'No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law' (Article 50 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU). Discuss critically, by reference particularly to Article 50 of the Charter and to relevant case law of the Union judicature, the scope and application of the principle of ne bis in idem in EU law.

questions.description: Article 119(2) TFEU provides that the activities of the Union 'shall include a single currency, the euro, and the…
questions.text: Article 119(2) TFEU provides that the activities of the Union 'shall include a single currency, the euro, and the definition and conduct of a single monetary policy and exchange-rate policy the primary objective of both of which shall be to maintain price stability' and that 'without prejudice to this objective', the Union's monetary policy shall 'support the general economic policies in the Union'. Discuss by reference to relevant case law in particular the judgment of the Court of Justice in Case C-34-09 Pringle v Government of Ireland, the scope and limits of the exclusive Union competence with regard to monetary policy. To what extent is that competence limited by the express prohibitions on monetary financing contained in Article 123(1) TFEU and on the assumption of the commitments of Member States and/or the public authorities in Article 125(1) TFEU?

questions.description: Consider the following fictional situation which is based loosely on that considered by the Court for Justice in Case…
questions.text: Consider the following fictional situation which is based loosely on that considered by the Court for Justice in Case C-244/13 Ogieriakhi v Minister for Justice and Equality (Judgment of 10 July 2014 : C: 2014: 2068). Edward, a Somalian national, came to Ireland fleeing war torn Somalia in 1998 and claimed asylum. A year later he married a Spanish national, Maria, withdrew his asylum application and was granted a one year residence permit on the basis of his marriage to Maria. This enabled Edward to fins work with a semi-state body. For the next three years, Edward lived with Maria, who, having initially come to Ireland in 1998, was an employee until June 2004. Each year, Edward's residence permit was renewed for a further year. In June 2001, Maria moved out of the family home to take up residence with a woman. Subsequently, Edward moved out of to reside with Deirdre, an Irish national, with whom he subsequently had an Irish National child, born in December 2005. From June 2001, Maria played no part in the provision or the making available of housing for Edward and in June 2004 when her relationship with her female partner broke down, she returned to Spain. In May 2005, the Minister for Justice and Equality refused to renew Edward's residence permit on the basis that he could no longer show that Maria was exercising her EU rights in Ireland and/or providing him with housing. In May 2006, Edward submitted an application for a permanent right of residence contending that the right to permanent residence, conferred by Articles 16 and 18 of Directive 2004/38/EC of the EU Parliament and of the Council of 29 April 2004 on the right of citizens of the Union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States, could be invoked by family members like him, who have legally resided in a host ember State for a continuous period of five years. Edward claimed that he satisfied this requirement with regard to the period prior to this deadline for transposition of that Directive i.e. 30 April 2006. The Minister rejected this application in May 2007 on the basis that Edward could not rely on periods of residence prior to 30 April 2006 as well as on the basis that, since Maria had neither resided with nor provided housing for Edward since June 2001, he was no longer entitled to a residence permit under the previously applicable Article 10 of Regulation No 1612/68 of the Council of 15 October 1968 on freedom of movement for workers within the Community. Edward challenged this decision unsuccessfully in judicial proceedings in the High Court. Edward appealed but withdrew his appeal, when the State agreed not to pursue him for the costs of the High Court if he withdrew it. However, on learning subsequently of the judgment of the Court of Justice in Case C-162/09 Lassal, Edward sought leave from the Supreme Court to re-enter his appeal. The Supreme Court refused this request and a linked request for a preliminary reference. In May 2012, Edward also applied to the Minister for Justice and Equality to reconsider his May 2007 decision in the light of Lassal judgment. In May 2014, the Minister finally took a decision with regard to this application and granted Edward a right of permanent residence but based this decision not on EU law but on the exercise of her discretion under Irish law having regard particularly to his Irish National child. In December 2005, Edward was dismissed from his job with his semi-state employer on the sole basis that he no longer had a right of residence in Ireland. He was unable to work again and became dependent upon his partner Deirdre until May 2014. Edward has sought your advice as an EU law specialist regarding whether: He was entitled to the right to permanent residence conferred by Directive 2004/38/EC based on his residence prior to 30 April 2006 and if so, whether; The decision of his former employer based on the Ministers refusal to grant him a residence permit may be challenged and, if so, on what basis; He would have an entitlement to compensation and, if so, on what basis and from whom and for what could damages be sought?

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of ANY TWO of the following three (hypothetical)… Injunction, Shareholder.
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of ANY TWO of the following three (hypothetical) measures and practices with Union law, particularly (as relevant) with Articles 18 and 20-21, 34, 36, 45, 49, 56 and 63 TFEU and provide the required legal advices to the clients concerned: (i) A national law that requires all bread bakeries or those engaged in commercial break baking activities to have a 'bread-baking' licence and which subjects the grant of such a licence to compliance with a number of requirements including having a specified area for kneading bread, using organic flour in baking and paying an annual 'artisanal' levy to the 'Artisanal Bakers Association'. Under this law, 'FE-1 Value' a supermarket, has been prosecuted for producing, without possessing a bread-baking licence, fresh bread from bought in frozen dough at the bakery counters of its various retail premises. Furthermore, the local 'Artisanal Bakers Association' has brought civil proceedings against FE-1 Value seeking back contributions with interest thereon of the 'artisanal' levy. Advise FE-1 Value as to whether it could have a defence based on EU law to the prosecution and to suit for the back levies, referring to relevant Treaty provisions and case law; (ii) A national law in Member State A that requires an airline, which has an operating license from Member State B for scheduled and chartered flights i.e. commercial flights in non-scheduled traffic to obtain a discrete authorisation for each onward charter flight from third (i.e. non-member) countries into the territory of Member State A in circumstances where airlines authorised in Member State A for scheduled and charter flights are not subject to any further requirements with regards to such charter flights into Member State A from third countries. FE1 Jet Management has been fined by the Transport Regulator of Member State A for operating charter flights from third countries without authorisations for the flights in question. Advise FE1 Jet Management whether, based on EU law, it could challenge before the courts of Member State A the imposition of the fines and seek an injunction against future application of the requirement to obtain flight authorisations. (iii) John and Mary are residents of Member State C with a portfolio of investments in investment funds that are established both in that Member State and in other Member States, Member State C's 'Law on Investment Tax' imposes 'transparency' requirements on all resident and non-resident investment funds requiring them to communicate to their shareholders in the official language of Member State C, various items of information prescribed by the law and to publish the information concerned in that State's electronic bulletin for official announcements, accompanied by a certificate issued by a tax professional authorised by the Law to provide tax advisory services confirming that the information was established in accordance with the Law. Failure by funds to comply with these 'transparency' requirements results in investors resident in Member State C being taxed each year on the basis of receiving a dividend deemed to be worth 25% of the total value of their investment holding, instead of on the basis of the actual dividends received by them. John and Mart consider that, although the flat rate tax mechanism under the Law applies equally to 'non-transparent' resident and non-resident investment funds, it leads to indirect discrimination against non-resident funds. This is because, although resident funds generally meet the 'transparency' requirements of the law, this is not the case regarding non-resident funds. Advise John and Mary of the possible basis in EU law upon which the requirements of the Law could be challenged.

questions.description: (A) As an expert in EU private international law, you have been requested… Damages, Regulation.
questions.text: (A) As an expert in EU private international law, you have been requested to advise the following clients: (i) Gertrude is a professional photographer domiciled in Member State A, specialising in photographic works depicting the buildings of certain Austrian architects, including Georg. With permission from Gertrude, Georg used some of Gertrude's photographs in order to illustrate his buildings at a conference organised in Member State B by an international development construction firm, FE1 Limited which has its seat in Member State C. Following the conference, and without permission from Gertrude and without providing a statement of Authorship, FE1 Limited made the photographs used by Georg at the conference available on its website for viewing and downloading. Taking the view that her copyright had been infringed by FE1 Limited, Gertrude has brought an action for damages against FE1 Ltd before the Commercial Court of Member State A. FE1 Ltd has asked you for advice as to whether it would have any basis in Council Regulation No 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial matters for contesting the jurisdiction of the courts of Member State A and, if not, as to what if any, might be the limits of the damage for which compensation could be sought in those courts by Gertrude. (ii) Blackhall Air in Member State D which is an authorised airline having its seat in Member State D, considers itself to have been a victim of a cartel between two public-owned airport undertakings (Dire and Dastardly) in Member State E, both of whom conspired to fix airport charges at the several airports in Member State E used by Blackhall Air. The existence of the cartel has been established by a formal EC decision which both Dire and Dastardly challenged unsuccessfully before the Union judicature. Blackhall Air has sought your advice as to whether (a) it could rely upon Regulation 44/2001 to bring an action for damages for compensation for breach of Article 101 TFEU before the courts of Member State D, notwithstanding that Dire and Dastardly acted under public law conferred powers in Member State E in fixing the relevant airport charges and (b) if it successfully convinced the courts of Member State D to take jurisdiction and grant it judgment, the enforcement of that judgment in Member State E against the assets of Dire and Dastardly could be resisted on public policy grounds. OR (B) Recital 12 in the preamble to Council Regulation (EC) 2201/2003 PF 27 November 2003 on the recognition and enforcement of judgments in matrimonial matters and matters of parental responsibility states: The grounds of jurisdiction in matters of parental responsibility established in the present Regulation are shaped in the light of the best interests of the child, in particular on the criterion of proximity...' By reference to relevant case law interpreting Regulation 2201/2003, assess how effect has been given in practice to the 'best interest of the child' particularly in alleged child abduction or wrongful retention cases.

questions.description: Answer ONE only of the following three parts. Regulation, Company.
questions.text: Answer ONE only of the following three parts. Do not attempt more than one part. (A) All electrical power stations operating on coal in Member State F belonged to the national electricity company, FE. A competing electricity generator has complained to the commission regarding the favourable position conferred by law in Member State F on FE. The Commission identified two relevant markets -- the first being the supply of lignite and the second being the wholesale electricity market, which concerns the generation and supply of electricity and power stations and the importation of electricity by means of interconnection systems. The Commission has found in a formal decision that FE holds a dominant position on both of those markets. Furthermore, FE was granted exploitation rights for 91% of public deposit of coal in Member State F for which rights were granted. The Commission found that by granting to FE and maintaining in its favour quasi monopolistic coal exploration rights, Member State F had created an inequality of opportunity between economic operators in the wholesale electricity market and distorted competition, such that the mere exercise by FE of its rights would invariably lead to abuse of its dominant position. You have been asked, as a specialist in EU Competition law, to advise the Attorney General of Member State F whether and on what basis the Commission decision could be challenged as well as regards the likelihood of such a challenge being successful. OR (B) Critically assess the approach taken by the Commission and by the Union judicature in reviewing Commission decisions with regard to applying substantive test in force since 1 May 2004 under Articles 2(2) and 2(3) of the Merger Control Regulation No 139/2004, formerly Council Regulation EEC No 4064/89 i.e. that a proposed merger must 'significantly impede effective competition in the common market or in a substantial part of it' before the commission may block or clear it subject to undertakings. OR (C) Discuss, be reference to relevant case law of the Union judicature, the view that Articles 107 and 108 TFEU reserve a central role for the Commission in determining whether financial or other assistance that may constitute State aid is incompatible with the internal market. Consider the extent, also by reference to relevant case-law, to which the aforesaid central role is limited or restricted by the power conferred upon the Council in the area of State aid by the third subparagraph of Article 108(2) TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case note on ANY THREE of the following ten judgments setting out in each case note the core facts, the key…
questions.text: Write a case note on ANY THREE of the following ten judgments setting out in each case note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Union judicature. (i) Case 22/70 Commission v Council [1971] ECR 263; (ii) Case C-50/00 P Union de Pequenos Agricultores (UPA) v Council [2002] ECR I-6677; (iii) Case C-200/02 Zhu & Chen [2004] ECR I-9925; (iv) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II-3601 (v) Case T-342/07 Ryanair Holdings v Commission [2010] ECR II-3457; (vi) Joined Cases C-274/11 and C-295/11 Spain & Italy v Council [2013] ECR I-0000 (Judgment of 16/04/2013) (vii) Joined Cases C-584/10 P, C-593/10 P and C-595/10 P Commission, UK and Council v Kadi [2013] ECR 1-000 (Judgment of 18/07/2013) (viii) Case C-131/12 Google Spain SL & Google Inc v Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos & Mario Costeja Gonzalez [2014] ECR 1-000 (Judgment of 13/05/2014: ECLI: EU: C: 2014:317) (ix) Joined Cases C-293/12 and C-594/12 Digital Rights Ireland v Minister for Communications, Marine and Natural Resources & Ors and Karntner Landesregierung & Ors (Judgment of 08/04/2014: ECLI: EU:C:2014:238) (x) Case C-333/13 Dano & Dano v Jobcentre Liepzig.

questions.description: Answer part (A) and part (B) (A) Outline and discuss the key differences between the legal nature of (i) regulations…
questions.text: Answer part (A) and part (B) (A) Outline and discuss the key differences between the legal nature of (i) regulations (ii) directives and (iii) decisions in European Union Law as set out in Article 288 TFEU; (B) What are the general principles of European Union Law and how are they used in the interpretation and application of the European Union legal order?

questions.description: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European…
questions.text: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European Parliament in the legislative process that should satisfactorily address any outstanding concerns that may have existed about a lack of democratic decision making in the creation of European Union laws?

questions.description: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his opinion on…
questions.text: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his opinion on Case C-50/2000 UPA v Council; that: '[...] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by a community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, a substantial adverse effect on his interests'. He was clearly articulating that there was a lack of effective judicial protection for non-privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union's institutions. Explain briefly the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing here. Also, make an assessment of whether these concerns have been fully addressed by the changes that were made to Article 230 EC by what is now contained in Article 263 TFEU and related case law.

Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Part (A) The Court of Justice in Case C-603/2010 Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Union law when it stated in paragraph 23 of the Judgement that: '[...] in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order'. How have these principles of 'equivalence' and 'practical possibility' referred to above been developed by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law. OR Part (B) Article 6 of (the fictional) Directive 2012/1210/EU on the use of mobile phone technology in cars and motor vehicles provides that: '[all] cars or motor vehicles, whether used or new, sold by any undertaking or other operator after the implementation of this Directive must include, as a minimum safety requirement [...] a 'hands free', 'bluetooth' or similar capability for using the device in that private car or vehicles which provides the person(s) in control of that private car or vehicle with the possibility of orally communicating through their mobile device without having to hold it in their hand'. Article 29 of the same Directive further provides that: '[member] States shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 16 June 2014'. Sonia was knocked down by a car, which was being driven by Graham, in January 2015 while she was out walking her dog. The driver of the car, Graham, mounted the footpath while holding and dialling a number on his mobile phone at the time of his collision with Sonia. Sonia suffered bad injuries to her neck and spine in the accident. She has to give up her job as a physiotherapist as a result and she is finding it difficult to find alternative suitable employment. She is also undergoing a long and expensive course of physical rehabilitation. She was awarded €9,000 compensation paid by Graham's motor insurance company, but she insists that it only partially covers the losses that she has and continues to suffer. Graham has been convicted under the Road Traffic Acts of causing harm by dangerous driving and, as a consequence, was fined €2,000. He was also disqualified from driving for 2 years. He subsequently, and consequently, lost his job as a double glazing window salesman. Graham maintains that he would have used a 'hands free' function on his phone had his car been fitted with the equipment to do so. The car had not been fitted with any such device or capability. He bought the car, which was second hand and four years old in October 2014 from Reville Auto's Limited which is based in Enniscorthy, County Wexford. Directive 2012/1210/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Dangerous Driving (Use of Mobile Phones in Cars)(Amendment) Regulations in May 2015. Sonia seeks your advice on whether she can bring a claim for compensation against either (i) Reville's Autos Limited or (ii) the State for the non-application and/or non-implementation of the 2012 Directive.

questions.description: Part (A) The (fictional) Health (Consumption of Sugar) Act 2014 was… Advise the Department of Health.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Part (A) The (fictional) Health (Consumption of Sugar) Act 2014 was introduced by the Irish Government as part of State Policy designed to combat rising rates of obesity in the population. Article 9 of the Act introduces maximum permitted sugar levels in non-alcoholic beverages. It sets this at 10g of added sugar per 100ml of water used in preparation. Borris Beverages Limited produce a range of non-alcoholic drinks, including their most popular brand, Toxicola, which has an added sugar content of 18g per 100ml of water. Since the introduction of the 2014 Act they have reduced the sugar content used in Toxicola to the permitted 10g level but sales of the product have declined dramatically since then. Consumer research undertaken by Borris Beverages Limited indicates that people have stopped buying Toxicola as they do not like the new reduced sugar recipe. Borris Beverages Limited also imports a number of drinks from elsewhere in the European Union, including Limelov, which is made in France and contains 12g of added sugar per 100ml of water. They have continued to import and market Limelov in Ireland after the application of the 2014 Act, presuming that Article 9 of the legislation only applies to domestically produced products but they have now been warned that the continued sale of this product in Ireland may actually be contrary to the legislation. Borris Beverages have made a compliant to the European Commission about the application of the Health (Consumption of Sugar) Act 2014. The Commission has written to the Department of Health seeking reassurances from the Irish Government that the Act is in compliance with TFEU obligations on the free movement of goods. Advise the Department of Health. OR Part (B) Write a legal opinion on whether the introduction by the Irish Government of an additional €2 tax on the sale of every bottle of wine from off-licenses and restaurants may be contrary to Article 110 TFEU. You may presume that Ireland produces very little wine when compared to other alcoholic beverages that are not subject to the additional taxation.

questions.description: Colm is a 20 year old student who completed his leaving certificate school…
questions.text: Colm is a 20 year old student who completed his leaving certificate school exams in Ireland in June 2014. He has always wanted to study homeopathy and alternative medicine at university but he has not done well enough in his leaving certificate to get a place on any of the alternative medicine courses offered in Ireland. He did not want to repeat his leaving certificate in 2015 as he felt that he was already quite old for school, having taken a transition year option and having repeated his fifth year due to several long periods of absence when he was suffering from a virus that left him with very little energy. He later applied to, and was accepted for, homeopathy and alternative medicine at the New University of Amsterdam in the Netherlands. The course starts in October 2015. Colm moved to Amsterdam in February 2015 as he wanted to learn some Dutch and earn some money before starting his course. He worked for an average of 15 hours per week in a supermarket as well as one evening per week of voluntary work at a local animal hospital. He earned around €160 per week which covered his rent of a small bedsit and some other expenses but he did not save any money during this period and his income was supplemented by a gift of €75 which was sent from his mother every month. Given his low income, Colm will not be able to pay his fees or to support himself during his studies so he has applied to the Netherlands Student Support Scheme which offers low cost loans to students. His application was refused however, as he is not a Dutch national. Colm's virus recurred during July 2015 and he had to spend a week in hospital in Amsterdam. He has just been sent a bill for €5700 by the hospital as public medical care is only free in the Netherlands for non-nationals when they are in full time employment. Colm has returned to Ireland in order to avoid paying his medical bills. He is however, very keen to get back to Amsterdam so that he can start his new course. Advise Colm as to whether he can (i) appeal the decision of the Netherlands Student Support Scheme on the basis that it amounts to discrimination against a non-national who is a citizen of another EU Member State and (ii) challenge the bill that he has been sent by the hospital.

questions.description: Make a critical assessment of the ways in which European Union law had been developed and applied to ensure that, where…
questions.text: Make a critical assessment of the ways in which European Union law had been developed and applied to ensure that, where possible, men and women are treated equally in employment. Your answer should refer to the provisions set out in: (i) Article 157 TFEU; and (ii) related secondary legislation; and (iii) the most significant related case-law of the Court of Justice.

questions.description: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments, setting out in each case- note the core facts, the key…
questions.text: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments, setting out in each case- note the core facts, the key issues of EU Law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Union Judicature: - Case T-201/2004, Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II 3601; - Case C-370/2012 Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI756; - Joined cases 56 and 58/1964 Consten and Grundig v Commission [1966] ECR 299; - Case C-200/2002 Zhu and Chen [2004] ECR I-9925; - Case T-342/2007, Ryanair Holdings v Commission [2010] ECR II-3457; - Case C-236/2009 Association Belge des Consommateurs Test- Achats and others v Council [2011] ECR I-773; - Case C-384/1993 Alpine Investments v Minister van Faincien [1995] ECR I-1141; - Case 81/1987, The Queen v HM Treasury ex parte Daily Mail and General Trust Plc [1988] ECR 5483.
